symptoms.symptom_name: Recurrent atraumatic subluxation of left glenohumeral joint and right patella, spontaneously reducing
symptoms.onset: acute onset ~4 hours ago on minor movement

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Atraumatic, self-reducing, recurrent subluxation is the hallmark of a hypermobility flare.
Spontaneous relocation with no residual deformity argues against a persistent (unreduced) dislocation — though remote confirmation is not possible.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe secondary protective muscle spasm (trapezius/deltoid and quadriceps/retinacular)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reactive myofascial spasm 'splinting' unstable joints is the dominant driver of pain and immobility in a hEDS flare.

symptoms.symptom_name: Distal neurovascular status intact — no numbness, weakness, coldness, pallor, or cyanosis of hand/foot

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A negative patient-observable distal neurovascular screen is required to exclude limb-threatening compromise; if it changes, immediate escalation is warranted.
Intact distal neurovascular function makes a significant unreduced dislocation less likely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Afebrile with no hot, red, acutely swollen single joint

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever and of a hot/red/swollen monoarthritis is required to exclude septic arthritis clinically.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A high Beighton score confirms generalised hypermobility underpinning the phenotype — a clinician examination finding, sealed from telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Shoulder relocated with normal contour and intact distal pulses; knee centrally tracking with mild effusion and medial retinacular tenderness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal joint contour with intact distal pulses on examination argues strongly against a persistent dislocation — but is a clinician finding not available over chat.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Mild effusion and retinacular tenderness reflect the microtrauma of recurrent subluxation.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Mild tachycardia is a non-specific response to severe pain and distress; a patient-offered wearable reading, not a discriminating sign.
Isolated mild tachycardia without fever or systemic features does not indicate sepsis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Functional immobility — unable to transfer or self-care, living alone

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Marks flare severity and drives the same-day welfare/support need rather than discriminating between diagnoses.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Atraumatic self-reducing multi-joint subluxation plus severe reactive muscle spasm on a background of generalised hypermobility together constitute a characteristic hEDS musculoskeletal flare — recognising the constellation is what prevents over-triage of the alarming individual features.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person joint examination (reduction, distal pulses and neurology, effusion)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Same-day in-person assessment is the appropriate way to confirm the joints are reduced and neurovascularly intact — this cannot be established over chat.
Occult labral or meniscal/intra-articular injury from subluxation cannot be excluded clinically, but does not require acute imaging; arrange as an outpatient if symptoms persist.
Reasonable if there is doubt about reduction or concern for a bony injury; not mandatory when contour is normal and neurovascular status intact.
No clinical features of joint infection (afebrile, no hot/red/swollen joint) — aspiration and septic work-up would be over-investigation of a mechanical flare.
